content.full_text:
MWD
METROPOLITAN WATER DISTRICT OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA

Office of General Counsel

March 13, 1989

Lilly T. Spitz, Esq.
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Spitz:

Conflict of Interest Code of The Metropolitan
Water District of Southern California

Thank you for agreeing to attempt to resolve our
differences over the "$250-million exception" prior to going to
the full commission. A copy of the exception, contained in
section 7200 (b), (d), and (f) of Metropolitan's Conflict of
Interest Code, is attached to this letter.

As I explained over the telephone, it is
Metropolitan's position that it is not foreseeable that an
interest in one of the large companies covered by the exception
could be affected materially by a decision of a limited powers
special district such as Metropolitan. In addition, we
disagree with your staff's assertion that there is no authority
for the Commission to approve such an exception, and feel that
the Commission acted appropriately in approving it in 1978 and
refusing its staff's recommendation in 1980 that it be
deleted. There have been no "changed circumstances," as
required by Government Code section 87306, which would justify
deletion of the provision now. We would not object, however,
to an increase in the level of assets and revenues necessary to
meet the exception, in light of the passage of time since the
exception was approved.

Description of Metropolitan

Metropolitan was formed as a public agency in 1928,
pursuant to the Metropolitan Water District Act, Stats 1927,
chapter 429 (repealed and reenacted, Stats. 1969, ch. 209, as
amended; hereinafter referred to as "the Act"). It is made up

1111 Sunset Boulevard, Los Angeles, California • Mailing address: Box 54353, Los Angeles, California 90054 • Telephone (213) 250-6000

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Lilly T. Spitz, Esq.                    -2-                    March 13, 1989


currently of 27 member public agencies, consisting of 14
cities, 12 municipal water districts, and a county water
authority. It is governed by a Board of Directors currently
consisting of 51 representatives of its member agencies. (Act,
§ 50.)

     Metropolitan is a limited-powers special district and
has no power, jurisdiction or authority other than as discussed
hereafter. It is essentially a provider of supplemental water
at wholesale and exercises no powers other than those
incidental to that mission. Metropolitan has no general
regulatory authority over water use once it is delivered to the
member agencies or over any other public activity. It has no
permitting, land use, or general planning authority.

     The primary purpose of Metropolitan is to "provide,
sell and deliver water at wholesale for municipal and domestic
uses and purposes." (Act, § 130(c).) This is done by
acquiring and importing water to Metropolitan's service area
and delivering it to the 27 member public agencies on order.
The District has two principal sources of water—Colorado River
water pursuant to contract with the Secretary of the Interior
and State Water Project water pursuant to contract with the
Department of Water Resources. Each member agency has an
entitlement to a certain percentage of the water available to
Metropolitan. (Act, § 135.)

     To further its purpose, Metropolitan may provide,
generate, and deliver hydroelectric power and electric power as
necessary or convenient, and may provide such power to other
public entities and to private entities engaged in the sale of
power at retail. (Act, §§ 137-139.1.) Metropolitan is further
authorized to acquire, construct, operate and maintain
facilities (Act, § 130(e)), acquire and dispose of property
(Act, § 140), levy property taxes (Act, § 124), enter into
contracts and retain such personal services (Act, § 122) as are
necessary or convenient to carry out its purpose.

     The Act authorizes the Board of Directors to fix the
rate or rates at which water shall be sold, but only as
prescribed by the Act. (Act, § 133.) That is, the rates must
be set so as to result in revenue sufficient to pay operation
and maintenance costs and bonded indebtedness. (Act, § 134.)
The Board may charge a different rate based on the source of
the water (Colorado River or State Water Project), but does not
currently do so. The Board is also authorized to provide
different rates for different classes of service, but the rates

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Lilly T. Spitz, Esq.                    -3-                    March 13, 1989


must be uniform throughout Metropolitan for like classes of
services. (Act, §§ 133, 134.)

     The Commission Has the Authority to
     Approve a $250-Million Exception

     The Political Reform Act (PRA) does not require
disclosure of all financial interests and, indeed, would be
unconstitutional if it did.

     By its own terms, the PRA limits disclosure to
financial interests which "may foreseeably be affected
materially by any decision made" by a designated employee.
(Gov. Code § 87302(a); emphasis added.) These limits on the
type of private financial interests which must be reported are
significant and are required by the Fourth Amendment of the
United States Constitution and article I, section 1 of the
California Constitution. An earlier version of PRA was
declared unconstitutional because it did not include proper
limits on the disclosure required. (City of Carmel-by-the-Sea
v. Young (1970) 2 Cal.3d 259.) A subsequent version was
considered proper, in part, because it did properly limit
disclosure to those interests materially affected.

     "More importantly the act's 'disclosure' provisions
are aimed at requiring disclosure only if the
official's interests could be 'affected materially' by
his public service." (County of Nevada v. MacMillen
(1974) 11 Cal.3d 662, 671.)

     Clearly, the PRA and the federal and state
constitutions limit the requirement to disclose personal
financial interests to those instances where it is foreseeable
that a decision could have a material impact on the interest.
The Commission is the entity with the authority (if not the
duty) to promulgate regulations and approve conflict of
interest codes which limit disclosure consistent with the
constitution and the PRA. (Gov. Code §§ 82011(a), 83111 &
83112.)

     It is Not Foreseeable that Companies
     Within the $250-Million Exception
     Would be Materially Affected by a
     Decision by Metropolitan

     To come within the $250-million exception, a company
must have $250 million in annual gross income and $250 million
in assets. These are only the very largest national

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Lilly T. Spitz, Esq.                    -4-                    March 13, 1989


companies. In addition to being a very large company, it must
not be in one of a number of specifically identified types of
industries which foreseeably could be affected by a
Metropolitan decision and which, therefore, are excluded from
the $250-million exception.

     In analyzing foreseeability, the Commission has
adopted the United States Supreme Court's approach. (In Re
Thorner Opinions 198, 203-271, citing United
States v. Mississippi Valley Generating Company (1961) 346 U.S.
520.) That is, certainty is not required, but something more
than a mere possibility of a material effect is required.
"Substantial probability," "likely," and "likelihood" are the
words emphasized in the Thorner opinion.

     As described above, Metropolitan is a limited-powers
special district with no authority other than that necessary to
operate and maintain its water delivery system. It has no
general regulatory role, no permitting authority, no land use
planning role or any other general authority or jurisdiction.
It is authorized, essentially, to operating a public
wholesale water delivery system. There is simply no
"substantial probability" or "likelihood" that the types of
decisions made by Metropolitan in fulfilling its limited
purpose and authority will have a material effect on large
Fortune 500-type companies, let alone a material impact on a
designated employee's financial interest in such a company.

     Decisions which foreseeably could have a material
effect on certain types of businesses (even though very
large)--pricing decisions with respect to private water
companies and large water users, contract awards for major
facilities construction, purchase of electric power from public
utilities--are reflected in the specific exclusions from the
$250-million exception set out in section 7200(d) of
Metropolitan's Code. Metropolitan's $250-million exception is
thus drafted to accommodate the public's right to disclosure of
financial interests which "may foreseeably be affected
materially," with the individual's right to privacy with regard
to financial interests which could only be affected in a "de
minimis" manner. (County of Nevada v. MacMillen, supra, at
p. 671.)

     Approval of the $250-million exception was, and
continues to be, an appropriate exercise of the Commission's
authority.

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Lilly T. Spitz, Esq.                    -5-                    March 13, 1989


     The $250 million exception was first approved in 1978
and was intended to include only the major, Fortune 500-type
companies. It might be appropriate to increase the minimums to
reflect an increase in the dollar value of those companies
since that time. Metropolitan would not object to a reasonable
increase in the minimum, and if the Commission does deem such
an increase to be appropriate, we would be glad to work with
you to set a new threshold.

     Please call me so that we can discuss this issue
further.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        Fred Vendig
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        James F. Roberts
                                        Deputy General Counsel

JPR:cwv
Encl.
LDCORRE2 #4657

MWD Administrative Code                                    § 7200

Chapter 2

DESIGNATED POSITIONS AND CATEGORIES
OF DISCLOSURE INDEX


Sec.
7200    General Provisions
7201    Officers of the District
7202    Administrative Services Division
7203    Finance Division
7204    Operations Division
7205    Engineering Division
7206    Personnel Division
7207    Public Affairs Division
7208    Planning Division
7209    Right of Way and Land Division
7210    Resources Division
7211    Office of the General Manager
7212    Legal Department
7213    Water Quality Division

§ 7200.  General Provisions.

(a)  Except as provided in Sections 7200(b) and (d), investments
in any business entity or sources of income listed in Column II
of Sections 7201 through 7213 are disclosable if:

(1) The business entity in which the investment is held is
of the type which has contracted with Metropolitan within the
previous two years; or

(2) The business entity in which the investment is held is
of the type which has furnished supplies or services as a sub-
contractor under any contract with Metropolitan within the
previous two years; or

(3) The source of income is of the type which has con-
tracted with Metropolitan within the previous two years; or

(4) The source of income is of the type which has furnished
supplies or services as a subcontractor under any contract
with Metropolitan within the previous two years.


Revised 6/1/88

§ 7200                          MWD Administrative Code

(b) Except as provided in Section 7200(d) investments in business entities and sources of income are not reportable if the reported annual gross income and assets of the business entity or sources of income each exceeded 250 million dollars for the year preceding the filing of any financial disclosure statement under this Code.

(c) Where real property is made reportable in Column II of Sections 7201 through 7213, designated employees within Section 7201(a) shall report as an interest in real property any undeveloped real property located within Metropolitan's jurisdiction. For this purpose, "undeveloped real property" is defined as real property for which there are no current water connections available from Metropolitan to the retail supplier of water and such water connections would be necessary to provide a water supply to the property, or for which larger water delivery facilities from Metropolitan are required before increased water service can be provided for a presently contemplated change in the use to which the property is put.

Where real property is made reportable all designated employees shall disclose any interests in real property within one mile of any Metropolitan land if, in the previous two years, Metropolitan has engaged in a real property transaction or a change in use designation of that land or if Metropolitan has under active consideration, such a transaction or change in designation affecting or involving that land at the time of filing.

(d) Notwithstanding Sections 7200(a) and (b), investments in any business entity or sources of income listed in Column II of Sections 7201 through 7213 which are private water companies, public utilities selling electric power to Metropolitan, companies providing (a) construction services utilized in water activities, (b) electrical equipment and supplies, and (c) engaged in real estate development, and (d) classified as large water users, are disclosable if held by the designated employee, regardless of any contractual relationship with Metropolitan at any time.

(e) For purposes of Section 7200(d), "real estate development" means investments in any business entity and sources of income which are entities or persons engaged in real estate development for residential,commercial, industrial or agricultural purposes within the jurisdiction of Metropolitan.

(f) For purposes of Section 7200(d), "large water users" means a business entity, in one of the following categories if it obtains water from

[The image appears to be a mostly blank page with very faint text that is largely illegible. There are some small marks or dots visible in the upper right corner and lower right corner, but no clearly readable text content that can be accurately transcribed.]

MWD Administrative Code                § 7200

a supply of which two-thirds or more is furnished by Metropolitan, in
which a designated employee has an investment or source of income:
(a) petroleum and petro-chemical product production, (b) metal products
manufacturing and processing, (c) public utilities producing electric
power and (d) commercial meat packing, fruit and vegetable produc-
tion, canning and processing.

(g) A designated employee must disclose that he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
in a business entity listed in Column II of Sections 7201 through 7213
doing business within Metropolitan's jurisdiction and which is of the type
which has contracted with Metropolitan or has furnished supplies or
services as a subcontractor under any contract with Metropolitan within
the previous two years.

Section 3611—M.I. 33561 — January 13, 1981, amended by FFPC to add cur-
rent Section 361.8.1.7 — effective May 6, 1981; Sections 361.1 through
361.8.1.7 — M.I. 32171 — February 14, 1978, amended and approved by FFPC
May 2, 1978; further amended by M.I. 33561 — January 13, 1981, amended and
approved by FFPC — effective May 6, 1981. Section 361.8.1 repealed and Sec-
tion 7200 adopted by M.I. 36464 — January 13, 1987, effective April 1, 1987;
amended by M.I. 36811 — September 22, 1987; amended by M.I. 37135 —
May 10, 1988.

Revised 6/1/88

MWD
METROPOLITAN WATER DISTRICT OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA

Office of General Counsel

September 13, 1988

Mr. Gregory W. Baugher
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Baugher:

Conflict of Interest Code of The
Metropolitan Water District of
Southern California

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California
has proposed minor revisions to its Conflict of Interest Code
(Code), primarily to reflect changes in the reporting
responsibilities of certain designated employees as a result of
periodic staff reorganization. In a response dated August 9,
1988, the Commission staff declined to approve the revised
Code, unless Metropolitan agrees to make a number of additional
revisions.

We are willing to accept a number of the revisions
suggested in the August 9, 1988 staff letter. However, we do
not agree that deletion of the "$250-million exception" is
required or appropriate. Hopefully, we can work this out at
the staff level. If not, we hereby request that a hearing be
scheduled before the commission.

Specifically, we respond to the suggestions contained
in paragraphs numbered 1 through 7 of the staff letter, as
follows:

1. We disagree that there is no statutory authority
for the "$250-million exception" contained in section 7200(b)
of Metropolitan's Code. The Political Reform Act requires
disclosure only of economic interests which "may foreseeably
be affected materially" by an agency decision. (Gov. Code
§ 87302(a).) Clearly, the Act contemplates limits to

1111 Sunset Boulevard, Los Angeles, California • Mailing address: Box 54153, Los Angeles, California 90054 • Telephone (213) 250-6000

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Mr. Gregory W. Baugher                    -2-                    September 13, 1988


disclosure where the interest in question will not foreseeably
be affected materially.  Indeed, both the federal and
California constitutions require such limits to disclosure in
order to protect the privacy of individual designated
employees.  (City of Carmel-By-the-Sea v. Young (1970) 2 Cal.3d
259; County of Nevada v. MacMillen (1974) 11 Cal.3d 662, 671.)
It is entirely within the Commission's authority to approve a
code containing such a limit, where appropriate.  (Gov. Code
§§ 82011(a) & 83111-83112.)

     The "$250-million exception" is appropriate with
respect to a limited-power special district such as
Metropolitan.  It recognizes that, with certain key exceptions,
actions by Metropolitan's designated employees cannot
foreseeably materially affect economic interests they may hold
in large business entities.  The key exceptions (including
private water companies, large water users, and electrical
utilities selling power to Metropolitan), which are detailed in
section 7200(d), can have such an effect and have been so
excepted.  The Code, thus, has already been tailored to obtain
an appropriate breadth of disclosure consistent with the
requirements of the Political Reform Act and the federal and
California Constitutions.  The Commission recognized this when
it first approved the exception in 1978 and when it denied its
staff's recommendation to delete it in 1980.  We believe that
the exception is still appropriate now, and we do not agree
that it should be deleted.

     We concede, however, that, since the provision is now
ten years old, it may be proper to increase the level of assets
and revenues necessary to meet the exception.  We are certainly
willing to discuss such an amendment.

     2.  We agree to amend section 7200(c) as recommended
to require disclosure of property within two miles of
Metropolitan land.

     3.  We believe that the addition of "business
positions" to sections 7200(a)(1) & (2) is not necessary
because that subject matter is covered in section 7200(g).
Nonetheless, we agree to make the suggested revision to those
subsections.

     The addition of "business positions" to section
7200(d), (e) and (f) is not appropriate because those
subsections deal with the $250-million exception, which does

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Mr. Gregory W. Baugher                -3-                September 13, 1988


not apply to the disclosure of business positions. We,
therefore, see no need to make the suggested revision. If the
$250-million exception is deleted from our Code, these
subsections would be deleted in their entirety, in any case.

     4.     This change is acceptable.

     5.     We have discussed this recommendation with our
treasurer, and while investment consultants are not used at
present, it is possible that they could be used in the future.
The addition of investment consulting services as a reportable
interest for treasurer and assistant treasurer appears to be
appropriate.

     6.     Metropolitan does not own or use any land in the
Bay-Delta region. All of our service area and all of our land
and facilities are some 300 miles away, south of the Tehachapi
mountains. The disclosure suggested is therefore inappropriate.

     7.     We will add the consultant category as you suggest.

     We would prefer to resolve the issue of the
$250-million exception with the staff, rather than taking up
the Commission's time. We urge the staff to consider such a
course of action. We do believe, however, that the exception
is appropriate and should remain in the Code, perhaps adjusted
to reflect inflation in the last ten years.

     If I can answer any questions or be of further
assistance, please call me at (213) 250-6316.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        Fred Vendig
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        James F. Roberts
                                        Deputy General Counsel

JFR:cvy
LDCORRE2 #4077
